training_background.coach_supervision_related_training_quali:
1. ICF-Accredited Coach Training Program (ACTP) – Professional Coach Certification
   - Institution: Hudson Institute of Coaching, Santa Barbara, CA
   - Dates: 2014-01-15 to 2014-11-21
   - Duration: 8 months (approximately 180 training hours + 40 hours of supervised coaching)
   - Level: Equivalent to ICF PCC-level training; focused on foundational and intermediate individual coaching skills, ethics, and reflective practice. This programme gave me my initial grounding in contracting, building trust, goal setting, and working with clients’ developmental edges.

2. EMCC Practitioner Level Accreditation (Individual Coaching)
   - Accrediting Body: European Mentoring & Coaching Council (EMCC)
   - Date Awarded: 2016-05-10
   - Duration: Portfolio-based accreditation, drawing on 125+ hours of supervision, 500+ client coaching hours, and documented CPD over 2 years
   - Level: EMCC Practitioner (Individual Coaching)
   - Notes: Required a detailed reflective log, supervisor reports, and evidence of ongoing professional development. This process strongly reinforced the importance of supervision and reflective journaling.

3. Team Coaching Certification – ORSC (Organization & Relationship Systems Coaching)
   - Institution: CRR Global
   - Dates: 2017-03-06 to 2017-11-17
   - Duration: 5 modules over 8 months; approx. 84 coach-specific training hours focused on systems and team coaching
   - Level: Intermediate/Advanced team coaching; preparation for ICF CCEUs
   - Content: ORSC Fundamentals, Intelligence, Geography, Path, and Systems Integration. Provided deep grounding in systems thinking, relationship constellations, and working with conflict and polarization in intact teams and leadership groups.

4. Advanced Team Coaching Practitioner Programme
   - Institution: Team Coaching Studio (UK) – virtual cohort
   - Dates: 2019-02-04 to 2019-09-27
   - Duration: 9-month blended programme (approx. 90+ learning hours including live virtual workshops, practice labs, peer practice, and group supervision)
   - Level: Advanced Team Coach Practitioner
   - Content: Team diagnostics, working with senior leadership teams, contracting with multiple stakeholders, designing team journeys over 6–18 months, and integrating supervision into team coaching practice.

5. Certificate in Coaching Supervision
   - Institution: Oxford Brookes University – Centre for Coaching & Mentoring, Oxford, UK (blended/online cohort)
   - Dates: 2020-01-20 to 2020-12-04
   - Duration: 1 academic year; approx. 120 taught hours + 40 hours of supervised supervision practice + academic assignments
   - Level: Postgraduate Certificate in Coaching Supervision
   - Focus: Supervision theory and models (e.g., Seven-Eyed Model, Hawkins & Shohet; Proctor’s tasks of supervision), ethics in supervision, working with parallel process, and developing a personal philosophy of supervision. My final assignment explored supervising coaches who are working with agile product teams in tech.

6. EMCC Senior Practitioner – Coaching Supervision
   - Accrediting Body: European Mentoring & Coaching Council (EMCC)
   - Date Awarded: 2022-03-29
   - Duration: Portfolio accreditation over ~18 months, including 75+ hours of supervision given, 20+ hours of supervision-on-supervision received, and evidence of CPD
   - Level: Senior Practitioner (Coaching Supervision)
   - Notes: Required detailed case studies of my supervision work with both individual and team coaches, highlighting ethical dilemmas, use of self, and impact evaluation.

7. Short Course: Group and Team Supervision Intensive
   - Institution: Ashridge Hult International Business School (online intensive)
   - Dates: 2021-06-07 to 2021-06-11
   - Duration: 5 days (approx. 30 hours) of live virtual training and practice
   - Level: Advanced CPD – focused on group and team supervision
   - Content: Designing and holding group supervision spaces (for up to 8 coaches), working with live team coaching material in supervision, using creative methods (sculpting, systemic constellations, timelines) and managing group dynamics within supervision groups.

8. Additional CPD and Relevant Training (selected)
   - "Ethics and Power in Team Coaching" – 1-day online CPD, ICF Pacific Northwest Chapter
     • Date: 2021-10-14
     • Duration: 7 CCEUs (Core Competencies)
   - "Working with Trauma Awareness in Coaching and Supervision" – 3-part webinar series
     • Institution: Association for Coaching (AC)
     • Dates: 2022-02-03, 2022-02-10, 2022-02-17
     • Duration: 9 CPD hours total
   - "Diversity, Equity & Inclusion in Coaching and Supervision" – 6-week online programme
     • Institution: Coaching for Equity Institute
     • Dates: 2020-09-01 to 2020-10-13
     • Duration: 18 hours
     • Focus: Exploring identity, privilege, and systemic bias in coaching and supervision relationships, with specific application to diverse, global teams.

Across these programmes and CPD activities, I have maintained an average of 30–40 hours of formal CPD per year since 2015, with a strong emphasis on team coaching, systemic practice, ethics, and supervision skills. I keep a detailed learning log that I regularly bring into my own supervision to deepen integration into my practice.

coaching_experience.how_long_have_you_been_practicing_as_a_c:
I began practicing as an individual coach in mid-2013, initially part-time while still working as an internal HR Business Partner at a mid-sized software company in Seattle.

- 2013-06 to 2015-12: Approximately 5–8 client hours per month (side practice), mainly internal clients referred through HR and a few external referrals (mostly early-career professionals in tech and non-profit sectors).
- 2016-01 to 2018-12: Transitioned to full-time independent coaching and consulting. Averaged 25–30 individual coaching hours per month, primarily with mid-level managers and emerging leaders.
- 2019-01 to present (2025-12): Individual coaching continues as a core part of my portfolio, though now at slightly lower volume due to increased team coaching and supervision work. I average around 15–20 individual coaching hours per month.

In total, I have been practicing as an individual coach for roughly 11.5 years. As of 2025-10, I have logged approximately 1,800+ individual coaching hours across about 240 distinct clients. My work has spanned sectors including SaaS/tech, healthcare, higher education, and mission-driven non-profits. I typically work with clients in 6–12 session engagements over 3–9 months, with a smaller number of longer-term engagements (18–24 months) for senior leaders.

My individual coaching now often intersects with team and organizational development work: for example, coaching a CTO while simultaneously coaching their leadership team, or coaching multiple cross-functional leaders in a matrixed environment. This integrated perspective strongly influences the way I supervise coaches who are working both one-to-one and with intact teams.

My first exposure to team coaching was informal and internal, starting in early 2016, when I facilitated leadership offsites and ongoing team development sessions for product and engineering teams in my former company. At that time, I would not yet have called it "team coaching" in a formal sense, but I was already experimenting with more systemic and inquiry-led approaches.

- 2016-01 to 2017-02: Approx. 1–2 team engagements per year, mostly internal, labeled as "team development" or "leadership retreats." These engagements typically involved 2–3 half-day sessions over several months, with a mix of facilitation, coaching conversations, and follow-up individual coaching.
- 2017-03 to 2018-12: Following my ORSC training, I began explicitly positioning my work as team coaching. I completed 6 distinct team coaching engagements (ranging from 4–9 months each) with cross-functional leadership teams (8–12 members) and 2 intact operational teams. On average, I spent 6–8 hours per month in direct team coaching during this period.
- 2019-01 to 2022-12: Team coaching became a major focus of my practice. I typically managed 3–4 concurrent team coaching engagements at any one time, each lasting 6–12 months. This included:
  • A 10-month journey with a global product leadership team spread across the US, Germany, and India.
  • A 7-month engagement with the executive team of a regional hospital network navigating a complex merger.
  • Several shorter 4–6 month engagements with agile delivery teams in scaling tech start-ups.
  I averaged 12–18 hours of direct team coaching per month in this period, plus significant design and stakeholder contracting time.
- 2023-01 to present (2025-12): I now balance team coaching with providing supervision to other team coaches. I currently run 2–3 substantial team coaching engagements per year, typically 9–12 months each, and a handful of shorter, targeted team interventions. Direct team coaching time averages around 10–14 hours per month.

In total, I have been practicing team coaching in a more or less consistent way for about 8–9 years, with approximately 700–800 hours of direct team coaching time across 35–40 teams. My work has increasingly focused on senior leadership and cross-cultural teams, which is one of the reasons I am investing further in team coaching supervision—to deepen my capacity to support other coaches working in similarly complex, multi-stakeholder environments.

I began offering informal peer supervision in 2018, meeting monthly with two fellow coaches from my ORSC cohort. While these sessions were valuable, I did not yet consider myself a formal supervisor and saw this primarily as mutual support and reflective practice.

My more formal journey into coaching supervision began in 2020 when I enrolled in the Oxford Brookes Coaching Supervision programme. The timeline is roughly as follows:

- 2020-01 to 2020-12 (Training Year):
  • Provided 25+ hours of supervised supervision as part of the programme requirements. This included:
    - 1:1 supervision for 4 individual coaches (all ICF credentialed at ACC or PCC level).
    - Group supervision for a small peer group of 3 coaches, rotating cases and experimenting with different supervision models.
  • Received regular supervision-on-supervision from faculty, focusing on contracting, boundaries, managing parallel process, and working with my own triggers as a supervisor.

- 2021-01 to 2021-12 (Early Post-Training Practice):
  • Formally launched a supervision offering within my business.
  • Carried 6–8 regular supervisees at any given time, mostly individual coaches (ACC/PCC) working in organizational settings.
  • Ran my first open-enrolment group supervision series (two groups of 5 coaches each, meeting quarterly).
  • Averaged about 6–8 supervision hours per month.

- 2022-01 to 2024-12:
  • Expanded my supervision practice to include team coaches and internal coaches within organizations.
  • Regularly supervised 10–14 coaches at a time, a mix of:
    - 1:1 supervision (typically 6–8 sessions per year per supervisee).
    - Two ongoing group supervision cohorts (5–6 members each, meeting every 6–8 weeks).
  • Averaged 12–16 supervision hours per month.
  • Successfully completed EMCC Senior Practitioner accreditation in Coaching Supervision in 2022-03.

As of late 2025, I have been practicing as a formal, qualified coaching supervisor for approximately 4.5–5 years, with around 500+ hours of supervision delivered (including both individual and group formats). Within that, an increasing proportion of my work (now roughly 40–50%) involves supervising coaches who work with teams and complex systems, which is precisely the area I wish to deepen through specialized team coaching supervision training.

In addition to individual coaching, team coaching, and coaching supervision, I have held several related roles that draw heavily on coaching skills:

1. Internal HR Business Partner / Leadership Development Specialist (Pre-Independent Practice)
   - Period: 2008-09 to 2015-12
   - Nature of Role: While not labeled as a formal coaching position, a significant proportion of my time (estimated 30–40%) was spent in a coaching-like capacity—supporting managers with performance conversations, career development planning, and navigating conflict within their teams.
   - Examples:
     • Regularly held 60–90 minute "office hours" where leaders could bring real-time people challenges and we would work through options using a coaching approach.
     • Co-designed and co-facilitated a "New Manager Essentials" programme that included small group coaching circles (4–6 participants) over 6 months.

2. Mentor Coach for ICF Credentialing Candidates
   - Period: 2017-06 to present (2025-12)
   - Description: I have acted as a mentor coach for coaches preparing for their ICF ACC and PCC credentials or renewals.
   - Scope:
     • Typically 3–5 mentees per year, each completing 10 hours of mentor coaching as required by ICF.
     • Focus on observed sessions, detailed feedback against ICF core competencies, and supporting mentees to develop their own authentic coaching style.
   - This work sits at the intersection of skills development and supervision and has sharpened my ability to give precise, compassionate, and developmental feedback.

3. Facilitator of Reflective Practice Groups for Internal Coaches
   - Period: 2019-01 to present
   - Description: I design and facilitate ongoing reflective practice circles for internal coaches within two client organizations (a healthcare network and a global NGO).
   - Format:
     • Groups of 6–8 participants.
     • 2-hour sessions every 6–8 weeks.
     • Blend of case discussion, peer coaching, and structured reflection prompts.
   - Although these are not always formally labeled as "supervision," the work is closely related and has provided a rich ground for developing my capacity to hold reflective, psychologically safe spaces for practitioners.

If I aggregate these "other" coaching-related roles, I would estimate an additional 600–700 hours of practice over the last 12–15 years that, while not always formally categorized as coaching or supervision, have significantly informed my approach to working systemically with individuals, teams, and the coaches who serve them.
